title: Coppice hazel plant supports
subtitle: Pea sticks, peastick bundles, and peony hoops from a coppice cut
excerpt: Hazel coppice produces three distinct kinds of plant support from the same winter cut: straight-tipped pea sticks from thin young poles, interlaced branch bundles for climbing plants, and bent hoops for peony and perennial borders. No tools beyond a billhook and a sloyd knife. All usable within a week of cutting.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for hazel coppice management and the use of the current year's growth for pea sticks. Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) for the kitchen-garden use of hazel sticks.

glossaryTerms.definition: Freshly cut, still-flexible timber at high moisture content. Hazel cut this season bends for hoops; it must be used within a few days of cutting before it stiffens.
A short section of coppice pole used as a support stake.

content.text: A winter coppice cut produces far more material than any firewood pile needs. The thin poles and brushy tops that would otherwise be chipped or burned are the best garden supports available: they are free, biodegradable, and structurally better than bamboo for the way climbing plants attach to them, because the twiggy side branches catch stems and tendrils naturally. This project turns the waste material of a hazel coppice cut into three types of plant support, all made within a week of cutting while the hazel is still

Freshly cut hazel coppice poles from the winter cut (November to March). Pea sticks: poles 10 to 12 mm in diameter, 90 to 120 cm long, from the thinner current-year growth with their branching intact. Hoops: poles 8 to 10 mm in diameter, 180 to 200 cm long, from the straightest second-year growth. Stake
: thicker poles 20 to 25 mm in diameter, cut to 70 cm, for driving as vertical stakes.
A billhook for cutting poles to length and pointing stake bases. A sloyd knife for trimming side branches flush and pointing pea sticks.

Billhook cuts away from the body on the upward stroke; the supporting hand holds the work above and behind the strike zone. Gloves are recommended for the non-billhook hand when working with fresh-cut poles with rough bark.

Select poles with branching over the top half of their length. Cut to 100 to 120 cm with a billhook. Point the base by placing it on a cutting block and taking two angled cuts to produce a stake tip; a point allows insertion without a hammer. Bundle in groups of ten and push into the soil around peas, beans, and climbing sweet peas. Space at 20 cm intervals and let the plants grow up through the natural branching.

Select the straightest, thinnest poles, 8 to 10 mm in diameter. Work within 48 hours of cutting: fresh hazel bends to a tight curve without snapping; after 3 days it has stiffened and will crack rather than bend. Bend the pole into a hoop of 45 to 50 cm diameter, cross the two ends, and secure by twisting the tail end around the main pole two to three times. Push three or four stakes around a peony clump and link them with horizontal hazel weave.

Cut the thicker poles to 70 cm, point with the billhook, and drive with a mallet. One stake per tall perennial (delphiniums, large salvias, standard dahlias). They will last two to three growing seasons before the buried end rots; hazel rotting underground is a slow process and the staked plant does not notice.
Birch twigs make finer pea sticks than hazel: the branching is denser and the scale is smaller. Useful for low-growing plants like sweet peas and nasturtiums.

A woven hazel low fence: push stakes at 30 cm intervals, weave thin flexible poles horizontally through them in an over-under pattern. Makes a low border divider that defines a kitchen garden path and lasts three to four years.
No care needed in use. Replace when the above-ground section shows significant splitting or the base rots at soil level. Compost the spent material.

items.cause: The pole was too dry when bent, or the diameter was too large (more than 12 mm).
items.fix: Bend hoops within 48 hours of cutting and use poles under 10 mm diameter. Thicker poles need steam to bend without cracking.

items.symptom: The pea sticks lean after a month
items.cause: The buried portion has lost moisture and shrunk from the soil, reducing grip.
items.fix: Push the sticks 20 cm into the soil rather than 10 cm. At 20 cm depth the soil contact is enough to hold against moderate wind load.
